Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 4 panels, each a 3x3 grid of 6x6 colored cells. Each panel's center cell has a special cross pattern with a 2x2 red square at its core. The bottom-right panel has a charcoal frame with gray corner decorations, making it visually distinct as the "active" panel. All cells in the bottom-right grid are blue.
- Goal model: Unknown yet - likely involves clicking the red center squares to trigger some transformation or matching between panels.
- Action model: MOUSE is the only valid action. Clicking likely interacts with the red center squares or colored cells.
- Plan: Examine all segmentation nodes to find the true interactive elements.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 4 of about 43 target actions so far.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 4 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see now! There's an orange (O) bar at the bottom - row 63, spanning the full width (64 pixels, bounds (63,0)-(63,63)). This is likely a timer or progress bar.
Also, the charcoal (c) node id=34 has bounds (32,32)-(61,61) - this is the large charcoal frame around the bottom-right panel.
The black (B) node id=0 is the background (2160 pixels, bounds (0,0)-(62,63)).
Now, I notice something important - the red cells have 36 pixels each (6x6), and the blue cells also have 36 pixels. The center cells have the cross pattern with smaller white/gray/red pieces.
But wait - I've been clicking and nothing changes. Let me reconsider. Maybe the puzzle isn't about clicking individual cells. Maybe I need to click on the entire panel or on specific pattern elements.
Looking at the image again, I see the bottom-right panel has a charcoal border with gray corner pieces that extend outside the border. These gray pieces are at the 4 corners of the frame. Let me look at their exact positions:
- id=33: center (34, 34) - top-left corner
- id=35: center (34, 59) - top-right corner
- id=62: center (59, 34) - bottom-left corner  
- id=63: center (59, 59) - bottom-right corner
Wait, I already tried clicking (34, 34) and nothing happened.
Let me think about this differently. The puzzle has 4 panels. Three of them have mixed red/blue patterns, and one (bottom-right) is all blue with a frame. Maybe the goal is to make the other panels match the bottom-right panel's pattern? Or vice versa?
But the bottom-right panel is all blue... and the other panels have different patterns.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the structure. The center cell of each panel has a special cross pattern. The three non-framed panels have different color arrangements around the center. The bottom-right framed panel has all blue cells.
Maybe the task is to click on cells to change their color, and the goal is to make all panels match some pattern? But my clicks aren't changing anything...
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to click on the orange bar at the bottom? Or maybe I need to click on the black background between panels?
Actually, let me re-examine the center cross patterns more carefully. Each center cell has a 2x2 red square, surrounded by white and gray elements. The pattern might be different in each panel. Let me compare them.
Actually, I just realized something. The cross patterns in the top-left and bottom-left panels look slightly different from the top-right and bottom-right ones. Let me check the ascii more carefully.
Let me look at the center cell of each panel in detail:
- Top-left center: has gray on left, white on top and right, gray on bottom with red center
- Top-right center: has white on all sides with red center  
- Bottom-left center: has white on left and right, gray on top and bottom with red center
- Bottom-right center: has white on left and right, gray on top and bottom with red center
Wait, let me look at the ascii output I got earlier:
Row 44-49 for bottom-left:
'bbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 44)
'bbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 45)
'bbbbbbccWWRRWWccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 46)
'bbbbbbccWWRRWWccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 47)
'bbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 48)
'bbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbbbcccc'  (row 49)
So the center cell has:
- Left: WW (white, 2 wide x 6 tall)
- Center: RR (red, 2x2 at rows 46-47)
- Right: WW (white, 2 wide x 6 tall)
- Top: gggg (gray, 4 wide x 2 tall at rows 44-45)
- Bottom: gggg (gray, 4 wide x 2 tall at rows 48-49)
This forms a cross/plus pattern.
OK, let me try a completely different hypothesis. Maybe the game is about clicking on the colored cells (the 6x6 ones) to toggle their color, and I need to match the pattern of one panel to another. But my clicks haven't been working...
Wait, maybe the issue is that I need to click on the exact center pixel of each cell, not just somewhere inside it. Let me try clicking on the center of a blue cell more precisely.
Actually, let me try something else entirely. Let me click on the orange bar at the bottom of the screen.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the orange bar at the bottom (row 63)
# This is the only O-colored element on the board
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 63, 'col': 32}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
